merged_gt.meeting_context: The Education Enhancement Reserve Joint Powers Board met to review district uses of Shoreline funds, confirm FY22/23 payment amounts, and discuss negotiating a successor agreement before the current JPA expires June 30, 2023. The Board aligned on a short-term payment guarantee and a Q1 2023 meeting while outlining a path toward a longer-term agreement.

topics.topic_merge_note: normalized_from: ['Administrative actions and minutes approval', 'Meeting Administration and Roll Call']

points.speaker: Kimba McCarthy
points.text: December 16th, 2021 meeting minutes were approved by board motion.
points.source: Kimba McCarthy : Minutes adopted

points.speaker: Kimba McCarthy
points.text: Meeting conducted under California Government Code Section 54953 subsection E during declared state of emergency with all members participating by video conference.
points.source: Kimba McCarthy : During this declared state of emergency, this meeting will be conducted in accordance with California Government Code Section 54953, subsection E

topics.topic: MVLA High School District funding activities

points.speaker: Nellie Meyer
points.text: MVLA High School District receives $3.204545 million in revenue from Education Enhancement Reserve with specific allocations for programs and staff.
points.source: Nellie Meyer : The NBLA High School District receives 3.2 O 4545 million dollars in revenue from this particular reserve

points.speaker: Nellie Meyer
points.text: $1.124 million supports Freestyle Academy of Communications and Arts Technology career pathway including teacher salaries and benefits.
points.source: Nellie Meyer : 1.124 million is currently used to support our Freestyle Academy of Communications and Arts and Technology, which is a career pathway

points.speaker: Nellie Meyer
points.text: $1.241 million funds expansion of computer science and engineering class teacher salaries and benefits.
points.source: Nellie Meyer : It is also used to expand computer science and engineering class teacher salaries and benefits for a total of $1.241 million

points.speaker: Nellie Meyer
points.text: $838,255 supports technology equipment and technology staff support services.
points.source: Nellie Meyer : our third bucket if you will, goes to 838,255 is used to support technology equipment and other support services, meaning technology staff

topics.topic: Mountain View Whisman District funding activities

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: Mountain View Whisman received over $5 million in base and bonus funds and spent $3.6 million total.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : So Mountain View Whisperman received a total of a little over $5,000,000 in base and bonus funds and spent a total of 3.6

points.speaker: Laura Blakely
points.text: Unused funds are reserved for textbook adoptions in future years, with math adoption coming up requiring $2-3 million each.
points.source: Laura Blakely : We've historically used the the we reserve a portion of them for our textbook adoptions

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: Funding supports Living Classroom, science programs, technology services, Chromebook software licensing, and fund administration.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : The funding received contributed to Living Classroom, science count, technology services, Chromebook software licensing, as well as administration of the actual funds

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: Large portion of funds maintains one-to-one environment by providing replacement Chromebooks and software across the district.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : A large portion of the funds received continue to fund our one to one environment by providing replacement Chromebooks and software that are used across the district

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: Living Classroom provides hands-on science lessons to TK through 5th grade students and maintains native plant gardens on each campus.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : Living Classroom provides hands on science lessons to TK through 5th grade students at the elementary level school in the district and helps to maintain native plant gardens on each campus

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: Property tax revenues increased 6.82% this year reflecting ongoing strength in real estate market, resulting in Mountain View School District receiving about $5.3 million and high school district receiving about $3.4 million, totaling about $8.7 million for upcoming year.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : for the current year, the upcoming 23 payment that increased by 6.82% this past year and is really indicative of the ongoing strength in the real estate market

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: Total payments since 2011 have reached $76.7 million, with cumulative payments including pre-2011 totaling almost $82 million.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : Since we've been been doing this back in 20, 11 have been $76.7 million dollars in total and including payments going back to fiscal 5 and six before the actual current agreements are in place

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: Growth expected to continue but recent economic conditions and layoffs could impact future assessed values through appeals process over several years.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : we do expect that the growth will continue. Of course we're only halfway through and anything can happen. However, we we do just want to be aware that the recent economic conditions that we've been seeing, there has been a slowdown, there have been layoffs that could impact future assessed values

topics.topic: Successor agreement negotiations and extension plan

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: Current Education Enhancement Reserve JPA agreement expires June 30, 2023, requiring negotiation of successor agreement.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : the Education Enhancement Reserve JPA agreement, which is set to expire on June 30th of next year, 2023

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: City proposed 12-18 month extension to complete analysis and continue payments at least equal to current levels during extension.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : I proposed the extension for 12 to 18 months to be able to continue payments to the districts to allow for the completion of this analysis and for the on ongoing negotiation

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: City proposes updating Shoreline Area Plan to enable expanded payments to school districts for school capacity needs over roughly 20-year development phase.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : our legal analysis has determined that to enable expanded payments to the school districts for school capacity will require that the shoreline area plan be up updated

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: Sobrato development portion available for occupation in mid-2024, providing timeline context for student enrollment impact.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : And the Sobrato development isn't a portion of that only and it would be available for occupation in 2024, in mid 2024

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: Staff working group began meeting in mid-2019 as prescribed in current agreement to discuss community obligations and district needs.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : the start of the staff working group, which began meeting in mid 2019, which is a term explicitly prescribed in the current agreement

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: City shared information about $122 million in capital improvements for sea level rise, $19 million for closed landfill improvements, and $487 million in transportation improvements.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : Regarding $122 million in capital improvements for sea level rise to protect both the North East Shore area and the broader city. 19,000,000 in improvements for the closed regional landfill to protect from leachate and methane gas leakage into the air and into the groundwater. 487,000,000 in transportation improvements

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: Long-range forecast depends on assumptions from Google Master Plan expected for Council adoption in spring 2023.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : the Long Range forecast, which we will use to illustrate the community's ability to fund both its obligations and the continued and increased payments to the districts depended on the assumptions that are will be part of an adopted Google Master Plan

topics.topic_merge_note: normalized_from: ['Enrollment impact and budget risks', 'District concerns and budget impact']

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: PAIR project with 635 units expected to produce 120 students requiring approximately $2.8 million in operational costs at current $23,000 per student expenditure.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : The PAIR project is supposed to it's almost finished which is 635 units. Our projections have it, which is very conservative, that eventually it will produce 120 students and as a result of our current, and this is just happens to be how how the cookie crumbled here. At our current expenditure of $23,000, that almost equates to $2.8 million in tax revenue

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: District budget cycle starts in February requiring planning for potential $2.8 million budget cuts without successor agreement.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : we start and I understand everything that occurred when we started in 2019 negotiating. But we our budget cycle actually starts in February which means that we need to go ahead and start presenting a $2.8 million budget cut because we don't have a successor agreement

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: District authorized to approve only six-month extension, expressing concern about timeline constraints.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : I'm only authorized to give up to six months extension

points.speaker: Nellie Meyer
points.text: MVLA district has no formal authority regarding timeline but shares concern about extension potentially meaning shift in leadership and variables.
points.source: Nellie Meyer : we do not have any formal authority as a district regarding a timeline. But there definitely is that same concern that any extension as it as it goes out could mean a shift in leadership and all sorts of different variables

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: County receives approximately 40% of what they would otherwise get, not their full allocation as clarified.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : So they are not receiving the full, their full allocation. It is really closer to about 40% of what they would otherwise be getting

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: County reached agreement in 2005 to receive full 20% share of tax increment starting fiscal year 2010-11, questioning different treatment from school districts.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : So in 2005, the county and the city of the Shoreline Regional Park reached an agreement that in 2010, 2011 that the county will receive full funding

points.speaker: Laura Blakely
points.text: Laura Blakely urged negotiating JPA first before Google development agreement, noting Google's $17.35 billion third quarter profits and ability to contribute to infrastructure.
points.source: Laura Blakely : I urge you to do exactly the opposite and negotiate with the JPA parties 1st and then press Google to foot the bill for more of the fancy infrastructure they'd like to see built rather than diverting Shoreline tax revenue funds that should be going to our schools

points.speaker: Pat Showalter
points.text: City Council member Pat Showalter committed to good faith negotiations and emphasized sea level rise as local responsibility requiring timely construction.
points.source: Pat Showalter : I can say that the city is committed to negotiate in good faith with both school districts. We are committed to coming up with a solution that meets the needs of our infrastructure and the school districts

points.speaker: Laura Blakely
points.text: Projected student population increase of 50% requires districts to receive full roughly 40% share of shoreline tax revenues for operational costs and new school construction.
points.source: Laura Blakely : What we did not know and would never have fathomed at the time the prior GPA deals were struck was that the projected student population of our schools, including students living in the new developments in N Bayshore, would increase by 50%

topics.topic_merge_note: normalized_from: ['Meeting closure and next steps', 'Future meeting plan and next steps']

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: Ayinde Rudolph thanked participants for willingness to work through complex obligations and continued partnership including teacher housing and joint use agreements.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : I I just want to thank you all for your willingness to go through it. I know it's it's this is a a change right and everybody has obligations

points.speaker: Kimba McCarthy
points.text: City committed to maintaining at least current payment levels during negotiations and expressed intention for collaborative approach.
points.source: Kimba McCarthy : it is the city's interest to pay the school districts not only the amount to have the certainty, the amount that you had received this past year, but of course any of those increases that may come about as part of the property value

points.speaker: Kimba McCarthy
points.text: Meeting adjourned at 12:14 with commitment to schedule next meeting in quarter one 2023.
points.source: Kimba McCarthy : So we'll adjourn at 12:14 and we'll just be in touch for setting the next meeting

decisions.text: Staff will continue negotiations and develop proposals for short-term agreement guaranteeing current payment levels and process for long-term agreement by quarter one 2023.
decisions.source: Audrey Ramberg : What I would hope for is that Mike and Rebecca and I would continue to meet and meet as frequently as necessary to take in the the interests and input that have been expressed at this meeting

decisions.text: Board will schedule meeting in quarter one 2023 to discuss short-term agreement before districts' budget planning deadlines.
decisions.source: Kimba McCarthy : So the Board has agreed to set a date for quarter one 2023 for our next meeting to discuss the, the short term agreement

decisions.text: Jesse will provide backup documentation showing county's actual share of tax revenues versus full allocation.
decisions.source: Jesse Takahashi : Sure we can. We we can provide sort of the backup for where we're getting our information

p2_skipped.text: City outlined Shoreline obligations informing payment capacity: $122 million in capital improvements for sea level rise; $19 million for closed regional landfill mitigation; and $487 million in transportation projects, with about $267 million net to be funded by the Shoreline community after development impact fees and community benefits.
p2_skipped.source: Audrey Ramberg : $122 million in capital improvements for sea level rise… 19,000,000 in improvements for the closed regional landfill… 487,000,000 in transportation improvements… an estimated 267,000,000 are net… to be funded by the shoreline community.
p2_skipped.speaker: Audrey Ramberg

p2_skipped.text: A council member stated the city's commitment to good-faith negotiations and emphasized sea level rise as a local responsibility, noting state and federal funds are competitive. She supported a reasonable extension to address infrastructure needs while meeting school districts' needs through a balanced solution.
p2_skipped.source: Pat Showalter : The city is committed to negotiate in good faith… We can't do that without the sea level rise protections… it's naive to assume that state and federal funding will be forthcoming… I urge you to consider reasonable negotiations that… involve an extension.
p2_skipped.speaker: Pat Showalter

p2_skipped.text: Board members aligned on drafting a short-term agreement guaranteeing at least current payment levels while negotiations continue, and to meet in Q1 2023 (February or early March) to review and approve it. Staff will map the process for a longer-term successor agreement, aiming for clarity by summer 2023.
p2_skipped.source: Kimba McCarthy : The Board has agreed to set a date for quarter one 2023… to discuss the short term agreement… we do intend to put forth the full amount that you all got last year… give us a little bit more time to finish the legal and the fiscal analysis.
p2_skipped.speaker: Kimba McCarthy
